title: QUIC RFC 9000 Path Validation Response and Retry Architecture
purpose: Describe the bounded runtime path that starts path validation on apparent migration, retransmits PATH_CHALLENGE frames while validation remains needed, sends one matching PATH_RESPONSE per received PATH_CHALLENGE, and closes on mismatched PATH_RESPONSE payloads without claiming the broader migration sender model.
design_summary: The runtime treats packets from an unvalidated changed peer address as candidate-path traffic, sends a PATH_CHALLENGE with an unpredictable eight-byte payload, stores that payload and a path-validation timer generation on the candidate path, and retries with a new payload when the timer expires and validation is still pending. Receiving a matching PATH_RESPONSE promotes the validated candidate path through the existing migration path model. Receiving a mismatched PATH_RESPONSE on that candidate path enters the existing local PROTOCOL_VIOLATION close path. Receiving a PATH_CHALLENGE on an active or candidate path emits exactly one PATH_RESPONSE datagram that echoes the challenge data and consumes only the path-local anti-amplification budget.
data_and_state_considerations: Path-validation state remains connection-local and path-local. Candidate paths own the latest challenge payload, send count, timer generation, and validation deadline. The response path does not add retransmission state or sender-ledger entries because PATH_RESPONSE is an immediate one-shot response, while PATH_CHALLENGE retry remains governed by the path-validation timer.
edge_cases_and_constraints: A validated candidate path must clear the path-validation timer and must not retransmit stale PATH_CHALLENGE payloads.
An abandoned candidate path must clear the path-validation timer and must not retransmit stale PATH_CHALLENGE payloads.
Each PATH_CHALLENGE retry must use a different payload from the previous outstanding challenge.
A PATH_RESPONSE must echo the received PATH_CHALLENGE payload exactly.
A mismatched PATH_RESPONSE must not promote the candidate path.
PATH_RESPONSE emission must stay one-shot and must not add sender-ledger or retransmission state.
The response path must respect active-path and candidate-path anti-amplification budgets.
risks: Treating the retry timer as a full migration scheduler would overstate this slice; it only covers the current candidate-path validation retry loop.
Adding retransmission accounting for PATH_RESPONSE would conflict with the one-shot response requirement.
Reusing a stale challenge payload across retries would weaken response correlation and path-validation proof.

supplemental_sections.heading: Scope
supplemental_sections.content: This design covers the existing runtime path-validation response and retry floor for RFC 9000 Sections 9.3.3, 13.3, 19.17, and 19.18. It does not claim automatic alternative-path search, preferred-address migration completion, ECN validation, generalized multipath scheduling, public API migration controls, or broader sender pacing and recovery policy.
